planting.note: Start transplants indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost or plant container-grown stock in spring in very well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; orange hummingbird mint is native to dry, rocky desert grasslands and demands perfect drainage and low soil fertility to thrive.

directions: Start transplants indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost or plant container-grown stock in spring in very well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly alkaline to neutral, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; orange hummingbird mint is native to dry, rocky desert grasslands and demands perfect drainage and low soil fertility to thrive. Agastache aurantiaca is a compact, drought-tolerant Southwestern native with aromatic, mint-scented foliage and dense spikes of brilliant orange to orange-red to bicolor (orange and yellow) tubular flowers from midsummer through late fall that are outstanding for ruby-throated and black-chinned hummingbirds; it is one of the most heat- and drought-tolerant agastaches and is an essential plant for xeriscape and hummingbird gardens in zones 6–10. The cultivar 'Apricot Sunrise' (with apricot-orange flowers) and 'Tango' (deep orange) are the most widely grown selections; plants are often short-lived but self-seed freely in well-drained soil.

planting.note: Start transplants indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost in very well-drained, dry, sandy to gravelly, slightly alkaline to neutral, poor soil in full sun; double bubble mint is native to rock ledges and dry slopes in the Chihuahuan Desert region and must have excellent drainage — wet soils are lethal.

directions: Start transplants indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost in very well-drained, dry, sandy to gravelly, slightly alkaline to neutral, poor soil in full sun; double bubble mint is native to rock ledges and dry slopes in the Chihuahuan Desert region and must have excellent drainage — wet soils are lethal. Agastache cana is a compact, long-blooming agastache native to the Chihuahuan Desert mountains of western Texas and southern New Mexico, producing narrow, intensely bubble-gum-and-licorice-scented leaves (the origin of its common name 'Double Bubble Mint') and dense, fluffy, 6–8 inch spikes of rose-pink to dark-pink tubular flowers from midsummer through hard frost that are exceptional hummingbird and bumblebee forage. It is among the most drought-tolerant and heat-adapted of all Agastache species; deer-resistant, essentially disease-free, and outstanding for rock gardens, dry borders, and xeriscape designs in zones 5–9.

planting.note: Start transplants after 4–6 weeks cold stratification (or direct sow in fall), in very well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; rocky mountain penstemon is one of the most adaptable western penstemons for eastern gardens if given excellent drainage.

directions: Start transplants after 4–6 weeks cold stratification (or direct sow in fall), in very well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; rocky mountain penstemon is one of the most adaptable western penstemons for eastern gardens if given excellent drainage. Penstemon strictus is a robust, upright western penstemon native to the Rocky Mountain foothills and sagebrush steppe from Wyoming to New Mexico, producing glaucous (blue-green), leathery leaves and 1–3 foot racemes of vivid blue-purple to violet-purple tubular flowers in June and July that are outstanding for native bumblebees and bumblebee-mimic hoverflies, and provide nectar for hummingbirds; it is one of the most reliable and vigorous of the blue penstemons in garden culture, adapting to a wider range of soils than most western species. Excellent in sunny, dry-slope rock gardens and xeric borders.

planting.note: Start transplants after cold stratification or direct sow in fall in very well-drained, dry to moderately moist, sandy to rocky, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; scarlet bugler tolerates heat and some humidity better than many western penstemons and adapts well to garden conditions across a broad range.

directions: Start transplants after cold stratification or direct sow in fall in very well-drained, dry to moderately moist, sandy to rocky, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; scarlet bugler tolerates heat and some humidity better than many western penstemons and adapts well to garden conditions across a broad range. Penstemon barbatus is the quintessential hummingbird penstemon — long (1–2 inch), narrow, vivid scarlet to coral-red, strongly reflexed tubular flowers on tall, wiry, branching stems in early summer through midsummer are perfectly adapted to ruby-throated hummingbird pollination, and individual flowers are often visited dozens of times per hour by foraging hummingbirds; it is native to dry, rocky slopes from Colorado to southern Mexico and blooms with extraordinary abundance and grace. More heat-tolerant and long-lived than many western penstemons; an outstanding early summer color element in dry pollinator gardens.

planting.note: Start transplants after cold stratification or direct sow in fall in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade to full sun; hairy beardtongue adapts to more diverse conditions than most penstemons and is one of the easiest eastern species to establish.

directions: Start transplants after cold stratification or direct sow in fall in well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade to full sun; hairy beardtongue adapts to more diverse conditions than most penstemons and is one of the easiest eastern species to establish. Penstemon hirsutus is the most adaptable and shade-tolerant of the eastern penstemons, native to dry to moist rocky soils, open woodlands, and forest edges across the northeastern and central United States; it produces sticky-hairy, soft stems and lavender to pale violet, tubular flowers with a prominent white palate (lower lip) in late spring to early summer, providing nectar for early queen bumblebees and the specialist Osmia penstemonorum bee. Unlike the dry-adapted western penstemons, it tolerates some shade and moderate moisture, making it more useful in the typical eastern garden; the dwarf variety 'Pygmaeus' is an outstanding rock garden plant.
